title: The Trimodal Nature of Software Engineering Salaries
excerpt: Why software engineering compensation follows three distinct peaks rather than a normal distribution, and what this means for your career.
content:
If you look at software engineering salaries in the United States, you'll notice something peculiar: the distribution isn't normal. Instead, it follows what I call a trimodal pattern, with three distinct peaks.

I've spent the last six months collecting compensation data from over 4,000 engineers across the industry — through anonymous surveys, public data from levels.fyi, and conversations at conferences. The pattern is unmistakable once you see it.

Peak One: The Workhorse Economy ($95K–$120K)

The first peak sits around $95,000–$120,000. These are roles at small-to-medium companies, non-tech enterprises, and agencies. The work is real engineering — building systems, shipping products — but the economic dynamics of these companies limit compensation.

Engineers in this band are often the most versatile. They wear multiple hats, touch every part of the stack, and develop broad skills. But they're working at companies where software is a cost center, not a revenue driver. The business model simply can't support higher compensation, regardless of how talented the engineer is.

"The salary you earn is not a measure of your skill. It's a measure of the economic value your employer can extract from your work. These are very different things." — Patrick McKenzie

Peak Two: Big Tech IC ($180K–$250K)

The second peak is at $180,000–$250,000. This is where most Big Tech individual contributors land after a few years. The jump from peak one to peak two is often the single largest salary increase an engineer will experience, and it usually comes from switching to a company where software is the product, not a cost center.

What's interesting about this peak is how quickly engineers reach it — and how long they stay. A strong engineer can go from $100K to $200K in a single job change, but may then plateau for years. The skills that got you to peak two (solid coding, good system design, reliable delivery) are necessary but not sufficient for peak three.

Peak Three: The Leverage Zone ($400K–$800K+)

The third peak starts around $400,000 and extends well past $800,000. Staff engineers, principal engineers, and senior managers at companies where each engineer generates millions in revenue. At this level, compensation is less about the labor market and more about the value you directly create.

Here's what the typical salary bands look like at this level:

Level              Base        Stock/yr     Total Comp
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Staff (L6)         $210-250K   $150-250K    $400-500K
Sr. Staff (L7)     $240-280K   $250-400K    $500-680K
Principal (L8)     $270-320K   $400-600K    $700-900K
Distinguished      $300-400K   $600K-1M+    $900K-1.4M

How to Move Between Peaks

Understanding this trimodal structure matters because it changes how you think about career decisions. The question isn't "How do I get a 10% raise?" — it's "How do I move from one peak to the next?"

The strategies for each transition are fundamentally different:

  • Peak 1 → Peak 2: Target companies where software is the product. Practice system design interviews. Build a portfolio of shipped projects that demonstrate scope.
  • Peak 2 → Peak 3: Develop organizational leverage. Lead cross-team initiatives. Build a reputation for solving ambiguous, high-stakes problems. Write and present your work internally.
  • Within Peak 3: Create compounding value — platforms others build on, architectural decisions that save millions, mentorship that multiplies team output.

The most common mistake I see is engineers trying to optimize within their current peak — negotiating a 15% raise at a peak-one company — when the transformative move is jumping to the next peak entirely. It requires more risk, more preparation, and often a geographic or lifestyle change. But the delta is life-changing.

The uncomfortable truth is that the market doesn't pay for effort or even for skill in the abstract. It pays for leverage. And understanding where the leverage lives in each peak is the single most valuable career insight I can offer.

commentData.author: Priya Chakraborty
commentData.content: This perfectly captures what happened to me. I spent four years trying to negotiate my way past $115K at an enterprise company before a friend convinced me to interview at a product company. Went to $195K overnight. Same skills, different economic context.

commentData.author: Marcus Webb
commentData.content: The salary band table is incredibly useful. One thing I'd add: at Peak 3, the stock component introduces a lot of variance. A Staff engineer who joined pre-IPO vs. post-IPO at the same company can have a 2x difference in total comp. Timing matters more than people admit.

commentData.author: Elena Kowalski
commentData.content: I wonder how this model holds up outside the US. I'm in Berlin and the peaks seem compressed — more like bimodal, with Peak 3 barely existing except at a handful of US companies with local offices. Would love to see this analysis for Europe.

title: The Crispiest Smashed Potatoes You'll Ever Make
excerpt: After testing twelve variations, I found the combination that gives you shatteringly crisp edges with creamy centers every single time.
content:
I've been making smashed potatoes for years, but I was never quite satisfied. Sometimes the edges would be perfectly crisp, other times they'd be sadly pale and soft. I wanted consistency — the kind where every single potato on the sheet pan is a golden, shattery miracle.

So I did what I always do: I made twelve batches in two weeks and drove my family slightly insane. Here's what I learned.

Choosing Your Potatoes

First, the potato matters more than you think. Yukon Golds are the classic choice, but small red potatoes — the ones about 1.5 inches across — give you a better ratio of crispy surface to creamy interior. Look for ones that are roughly the same size so they cook evenly.

Avoid anything too large. If a potato is bigger than a golf ball, it won't smash into a thin enough disk to get properly crispy. You want surface area, and small potatoes give you more of it relative to their volume.

The Boil

Put the potatoes in cold, salted water — I use about two tablespoons of kosher salt per quart. Starting in cold water ensures they cook evenly from the outside in. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a simmer and cook until a knife slides through without any resistance. This usually takes 15–20 minutes depending on size.

Pro tip: Slightly overcooking the potatoes is better than undercooking. If they're a little past done, they'll smash more easily and create more of those craggy edges that get impossibly crispy in the oven.

The Smash

Drain the potatoes and let them steam dry on the sheet pan for five minutes. This step matters — surface moisture is the enemy of crispiness. Then comes the smashing technique, which is critical.

Use the bottom of a measuring cup and press firmly but don't obliterate them. You want them about half-inch thick with jagged, craggy edges. Those edges are where the magic happens. Press down with one decisive motion — don't wiggle or twist, which can cause the potato to break apart instead of smashing cleanly.

The Secret: Starchy Oil

Third — and this is the real secret — toss them in a mixture of olive oil and a tablespoon of reserved starchy potato water. The starch creates an incredibly thin coating that crisps up like the best French fry you've ever had.

Here's the complete method, step by step:

  1. Place 1.5 lbs small potatoes in cold salted water. Bring to a boil, then simmer 15–20 minutes until fork-tender.
  2. Reserve 2 tablespoons of the starchy cooking water before draining.
  3. Spread potatoes on an oiled sheet pan and let them steam dry for 5 minutes.
  4. Smash each potato to about ½-inch thickness using the bottom of a measuring cup.
  5. Whisk together 3 tablespoons olive oil with the reserved potato water. Brush generously over each smashed potato.
  6. Season with flaky salt, black pepper, and garlic powder.
  7. Roast at 450°F for 25 minutes, flip carefully with a spatula, then roast 15 more minutes.
  8. Finish with fresh herbs (I like dill and chives), a squeeze of lemon, and more flaky salt.

The Finish

When they come out of the oven, they should look almost alarmingly dark and crispy on the edges. Don't be afraid of color — that deep golden-brown is flavor. Hit them immediately with flaky salt, a generous shower of fresh herbs, and a squeeze of lemon.

Serve them as a side, obviously, but honestly? I eat a whole pan of these for dinner at least once a week with nothing but a dollop of sour cream and some hot sauce. No shame.

The starchy-oil trick works on other things too — try it on roasted cauliflower or broccoli. But smashed potatoes are where it really shines.

commentData.author: Jamie Torres
commentData.content: Made these tonight and the starchy water trick is absolutely real. My wife, who is very hard to impress food-wise, said these were better than the ones at our favorite restaurant. I'm never making roasted potatoes any other way.

commentData.author: Aisha Patel
commentData.content: Tried using duck fat instead of olive oil and oh my god. Also, I toss mine with za'atar and sumac after they come out of the oven. The Middle Eastern version of this is incredible.

title: Hannah Arendt on Loneliness as the Common Ground for Terror
excerpt: How the philosopher's prescient 1951 analysis of isolation versus solitude speaks to our current moment of disconnection.
content:
In The Origins of Totalitarianism, published in 1951, Hannah Arendt drew a distinction that feels startlingly relevant seven decades later: the difference between loneliness and solitude.

Solitude: The Productive Withdrawal

Solitude, Arendt argued, is the experience of being alone with oneself — a state necessary for thinking, for the inner dialogue that constitutes our mental life.

"Thinking, existentially speaking, is a solitary but not a lonely business; solitude is the human condition in which I keep myself company. Loneliness comes about when I am alone without being able to split up into the two-in-one, without being able to keep myself company."

In solitude, I keep myself company. I can examine my own thoughts, argue with myself, test my ideas against my own moral sense. This is the foundation of what Arendt called "the life of the mind" — the inner activity that makes us fully human, fully capable of moral reasoning.

The thinker in solitude is never truly alone. They are engaged in a conversation — a real, demanding conversation — with themselves. And this conversation imposes its own discipline: you cannot lie to yourself as easily as you can lie to others.

Loneliness: The Collapse of Inner Life

Loneliness, by contrast, is the experience of being abandoned by all others — including, crucially, by one's own self. In loneliness, the inner dialogue ceases. We lose the capacity to think, in Arendt's specific sense of the word: to have a conversation with ourselves, to examine our own thoughts and beliefs.

"What makes loneliness so unbearable is the loss of one's own self which can be realized in solitude, but confirmed in its identity only by the trusted and trustworthy company of my equals."

This is not merely the feeling of being alone. One can be surrounded by people — at a rally, in a crowd, scrolling through a social feed — and still be profoundly lonely in Arendt's sense. Loneliness is a severing from both others and oneself. It is the experience of having no one to think with, including the person you should know best.

The Common Ground for Terror

This distinction matters because Arendt identified loneliness — not poverty, not injustice, not even hatred — as the "common ground for terror." When people are unable to think for themselves, when the inner dialogue has been silenced by isolation, they become vulnerable to ideologies that offer the false comfort of certainty.

Totalitarian movements, Arendt observed, do not primarily recruit the desperate or the angry. They recruit the lonely. People who have lost the capacity for independent thought are drawn to movements that provide ready-made answers, total explanations, and the illusion of belonging.

"The ideal subject of totalitarian rule is not the convinced Nazi or the convinced Communist, but people for whom the distinction between fact and fiction, true and false, no longer exists."

This is perhaps her most chilling insight: the goal of totalitarian propaganda is not to convince but to destroy the capacity for conviction altogether. When you can no longer think — when the inner dialogue has gone silent — you will believe anything, because belief and disbelief have become equally meaningless.

Arendt in the Age of Connectivity

What would Arendt make of an era where we are more connected than ever yet report unprecedented levels of loneliness? Where the tools designed to bring us together seem instead to have eroded our capacity for the solitude that sustains genuine thought?

I think she would note, with grim precision, that social media performs a peculiar trick: it offers the appearance of connection while preventing genuine solitude. You are never alone with your thoughts when your phone buzzes every few minutes. But you are also never truly in the company of equals — you are performing for an audience, not thinking with a companion.

The result is a new form of loneliness: surrounded by voices, unable to hear your own. Connected to everyone, intimate with no one. This is exactly the condition Arendt warned about — not isolation from others, but the inability to be genuinely present either to others or to oneself.

Arendt's work suggests that the antidote to this loneliness is not more connection but better solitude — the deliberate cultivation of inner life, of the ability to think for oneself. This is, she argued, not a luxury but a political necessity. A society of people who cannot think is a society ripe for domination.

Her collected essays on thinking and moral judgment, published posthumously as The Life of the Mind, remain essential reading for anyone trying to understand how individual inner life connects to collective political reality.

The task she sets before us is uncomfortable but clear: learn to be alone with yourself. Not lonely, but genuinely alone — in dialogue with your own conscience, testing your own beliefs, keeping your own company. It is in this solitude, paradoxically, that we become most capable of genuine connection with others.

title: Apple's Services Inflection Point
excerpt: Apple Services revenue is now growing faster than any hardware category. This changes everything about how we should analyze the company.
content:
Apple reported services revenue of $26.3 billion last quarter, up 18% year-over-year. That's not just a record — it represents a fundamental shift in what Apple is as a company.

The Old Bull Case

For decades, the bull case for Apple was straightforward: they make the best hardware, charge a premium, and enjoy industry-leading margins. The bear case was equally simple: hardware is cyclical, and eventually the upgrade cycle slows.

Both of these arguments are now outdated. They describe a company that existed five years ago. The Apple of 2026 is a different beast, and the numbers tell the story clearly.

The Revenue Breakdown

Here's how Apple's revenue segments have shifted over the past four years:

Segment          FY2022    FY2023    FY2024    FY2025    FY2026E
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
iPhone           $205B     $201B     $212B     $218B     $224B
Services         $ 78B     $ 85B     $ 96B     $105B     $118B
Mac              $ 40B     $ 29B     $ 38B     $ 42B     $ 44B
iPad             $ 29B     $ 28B     $ 30B     $ 33B     $ 35B
Wearables        $ 41B     $ 40B     $ 37B     $ 39B     $ 41B
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Services % Rev    20%       22%       24%       26%       28%

Services is on track to become nearly a third of Apple's revenue within two years. And because of its margin profile, it already accounts for an even larger share of profits.

The Margin Story

Services changes the equation entirely. Every iPhone, iPad, Mac, and Apple Watch sold becomes a recurring revenue opportunity. The complete services portfolio now includes:

  • App Store — still the single largest services revenue driver
  • iCloud — storage tiers from 50GB to 12TB
  • Apple Music — over 100 million subscribers
  • Apple TV+ — growing content library with award-winning originals
  • Apple Arcade — gaming subscription with 200+ titles
  • AppleCare — insurance and extended warranty
  • Apple Pay / Apple Card — financial services
  • Apple Fitness+ — workout subscription
  • Apple News+ — premium journalism bundle
  • Apple One — the bundle that ties everything together

Each service creates a monetizable touchpoint with users who have already been acquired through hardware purchases.

"Apple's services business alone would be a Fortune 50 company. We're analyzing it as a line item on a hardware company's income statement, and that framing is increasingly wrong." — an analyst who asked to remain anonymous

The Margin Differential

But what's truly interesting is the margin profile. Hardware margins hover around 36–38%. Services margins are estimated at 70–75%. As services grow from 22% of total revenue toward 30% and beyond, Apple's blended margin profile improves dramatically — even if hardware growth flatlines.

This is the part most investors miss. Apple doesn't need iPhone unit growth to grow earnings anymore. It just needs to sell enough iPhones to maintain and grow the installed base — the 2.2 billion active devices that form the foundation of its services business.

The Shifting Moat

The strategic implication is that Apple's competitive moat is shifting. It's no longer just about making the best phone. It's about building an ecosystem so valuable that switching costs become functionally infinite. Each new service adds another thread to the web.

Consider a family deeply embedded in the Apple ecosystem: shared iCloud storage, family Apple Music plan, kids on Apple Arcade, workouts on Fitness+, all bundled through Apple One. The monthly cost of switching isn't just the price of new hardware — it's the disruption to a dozen integrated services that have become invisible infrastructure in their daily lives.

This is the inflection point. Apple is no longer a hardware company with a services business. It's a services company with a hardware distribution channel. The sooner analysts update their mental models, the better they'll understand where this company is headed.

title: Your Daily Practice Doesn't Need to Be Daily
excerpt: The tyranny of consistency culture is making us miserable. Here's what actually works for sustaining creative work over years.
content:
Every productivity guru will tell you the same thing: consistency is key. Write every day. Draw every day. Ship every day. Build the habit, they say, and the results will follow.

I've been making things for twenty years, and I want to push back on this. Not because consistency doesn't matter — it does — but because the way we talk about it has become toxic.

The Shame Spiral

When "daily practice" becomes the standard, every missed day feels like failure. And failure, for most creative people, triggers shame. And shame is the single most effective creativity killer I know.

I've watched talented friends abandon entire projects — novels, albums, businesses — not because the work was bad, but because they missed a few days and convinced themselves they'd broken the chain. The streak became more important than the work itself. That's backwards.

"The creative process is not about grinding. It's about showing up with enough gentleness that the work feels safe to emerge." — a note I wrote to myself during a particularly fallow period in 2021

Rhythm Over Routine

What actually works, in my experience, is rhythm rather than routine. Rhythm is organic. It speeds up and slows down. It has rest built in. You might write intensely for three weeks, then read for one. You might sketch every morning in spring and not pick up a pencil all winter.

The key difference is that rhythm doesn't punish rest. It expects it. It builds in seasons — periods of intense output followed by periods of input, reflection, and apparent idleness that is actually essential processing.

What Perpetual Return Looks Like

Here's what I've learned about sustaining creative work over decades, not days:

  • Lower the activation energy. Keep your tools out. Leave the document open. Make starting so easy that it doesn't feel like a decision.
  • Protect your fallow periods. When the work isn't coming, read. Walk. Cook. The inputs during rest are what fuel the next burst of output.
  • Track months, not days. Did you make meaningful progress this month? This quarter? This year? That's the timeframe that matters for creative work.
  • Separate practice from production. Sketching for fun isn't the same as drawing for a client. Journaling isn't the same as writing an essay. Let some creative activity be purposeless.
  • Forgive yourself quickly. You stopped for a week. A month. A year. It doesn't matter. The work is patient. It will still be there.

The Real Practice

The work gets done. Not because you forced it into a daily box, but because you kept coming back. That's the real practice: not daily output, but perpetual return.

I think of it like a garden. You don't stand over your tomatoes every single day demanding growth. You water, you weed, you wait. Some weeks you're out there every morning. Some weeks it rains and you stay inside. The garden doesn't care about your streak. It cares about the long arc of attention.

Your creative work is the same. It doesn't need your guilt. It needs your patience, your presence, and your willingness to keep coming back, no matter how long you've been away.

commentData.author: David Chen
commentData.content: I needed this today. I haven't written anything in three weeks and I've been beating myself up about it. The garden metaphor really landed — I've been standing over my tomatoes yelling at them to grow.

commentData.author: Sarita Naidu
commentData.content: As a musician, this is everything. I used to practice scales every single day until I started dreading picking up my guitar. Now I play when I feel it and study music theory or just listen during the off days. My actual playing has gotten better, not worse.

commentData.author: Tom Brightwell
commentData.content: Counterpoint: for some people (myself included), the daily habit IS what removes the decision fatigue. I write every morning at 6am not because I'm guilting myself but because not having to decide whether to write today frees up energy for the actual writing. I think the key is knowing which type you are.

title: Chasing Light in the Dolomites
excerpt: Three weeks photographing the Italian Alps taught me more about patience than any workshop ever could.
content:
I arrived in the Dolomites with a shot list, a weather app, and a plan for every sunrise. By day three, I'd thrown all of it away.

Plans vs. Mountains

The mountains don't care about your plans. Clouds roll in when the light should be golden. The fog that obscured everything at 5 AM lifts at 5:47, gives you exactly eight minutes of ethereal perfection, then closes back in like a curtain. You can't schedule that. You can only be there.

I'd mapped out every location — Seceda, Tre Cime, Lago di Braies, the iconic little church at Santa Maddalena. I had GPS coordinates, sun-angle calculations, even notes on which compositions other photographers had already done so I could find something original. All of it was useless the moment I stepped out of the car into a wall of cloud.

The Discipline of Waiting

I spent most of my three weeks waiting. Waiting in cold meadows with a thermos of coffee. Waiting on ridgelines in wind that made my tripod vibrate. Waiting in the car with the heater running, watching the sky through a rain-streaked windshield.

"Photography is the art of observation. It has little to do with the things you see and everything to do with the way you see them." — Elliott Erwitt. I thought I understood this quote before the Dolomites. I didn't.

Waiting is not passive. You're reading the sky constantly — where the clouds are thinnest, which direction they're moving, whether that brightening on the eastern horizon will amount to anything. You're repositioning, adjusting compositions, running through settings in your head so that when the moment comes, you can shoot without thinking.

The Moment

And then, occasionally, the world would crack open. Light would pour through a gap in the clouds and ignite a mountainside in a way that made me forget I was cold, forget I was tired, forget everything except the simple imperative to press the shutter.

There's a particular quality of light in the Dolomites that I've never seen anywhere else. The rock is dolomite — a pale, almost white limestone that reflects warm light in a way that makes the peaks seem to glow from within. When the sun is low and the air is clear, the mountains turn a shade of pink-orange that the Italians call enrosadira. It lasts maybe ten minutes. It is, without exaggeration, one of the most beautiful things I have ever seen.

The Gear That Mattered

People always ask about gear, so here's what I actually used over three weeks:

  • Sony A7R V — the resolution was essential for landscapes where I wanted to crop and still print large
  • Sony 16-35mm f/2.8 GM II — lived on the camera 80% of the time
  • Sony 70-200mm f/2.8 GM II — for compressed compositions and isolating peaks
  • Nisi V7 filter system — 6-stop ND, polarizer, and 3-stop graduated ND
  • Really Right Stuff TVC-34L tripod — heavy but absolutely stable in wind
  • Thermos and hand warmers — honestly the most important gear on the list

The Ratio

I came home with 8,400 raw files and maybe forty images I'm proud of. That ratio — 210:1 — would have horrified me five years ago. Now I understand it. The waiting isn't wasted time. The waiting is the work.

Every one of those 8,360 "failed" images was part of the process — a test exposure, a composition that didn't quite work, a moment that was good but not great. You can't get to the forty without going through the eight thousand. The mountain teaches you that.

I'm already planning my return. Next time, no shot list. No plan. Just three weeks, a camera, and the willingness to wait for whatever the mountains decide to show me.

title: Why I Stopped Planning My Trips
excerpt: After three years of full-time travel, the best experiences have all come from the days I had nothing planned.
content:
When I first started traveling full-time in 2023, I was a planning maximalist. Color-coded spreadsheets. Hour-by-hour itineraries. Restaurant reservations made weeks in advance. I thought preparation was the key to a good trip.

Somewhere in Portugal, sitting in a tiny café I'd found by accident after getting thoroughly lost in Lisbon's Alfama district, I realized I was wrong. This — the unexpected, the unplanned, the stumbled-upon — was the whole point.

The best meal I've ever had was at a place with no sign, no menu, and no English. A woman in a small town in Oaxaca waved me in from the street, sat me at a plastic table, and brought out plate after plate of food I couldn't name. I still think about it weekly.

The most beautiful place I've slept was a hammock on a stranger's balcony in Medellín, offered to me when every hostel was full during a festival I didn't know was happening. I woke up to a view of the entire valley, golden in the morning light, with cumbia still playing somewhere below.

Planning creates the illusion of control. Unplanning creates space for the world to surprise you. And those surprises — the wrong turns, the missed buses, the language-barrier mishaps that end in laughter — are what I remember.

title: The Unbearable Lightness of Good Design
excerpt: Why the best design work disappears — and why that's the hardest thing for designers to accept.
content:
There's a paradox at the heart of design: the better you do your job, the less visible your work becomes. The perfect interface is one nobody notices. The perfect typeface is one nobody can name. The perfect user experience is one that feels like no experience at all.

This is genuinely difficult for designers to internalize. We spend years developing our craft, learning to see what others miss, training our eyes to detect the slightest misalignment — and then the goal is to make all of that invisible?

Yes. Because design isn't about the designer. It's about the person using the thing. And the person using the thing doesn't want to admire your grid system or your color palette or your thoughtful micro-interactions. They want to accomplish their task and move on with their life.

The best designers I know have made peace with this. They find satisfaction not in recognition but in the quiet knowledge that something works beautifully because of choices they made — choices that no one will ever see or appreciate. It's a kind of monastic devotion to craft.

Dieter Rams understood this. "Good design is as little design as possible," he said. Not because design doesn't matter, but because it matters so much that it should never get in the way.

title: What Reconstruction Can Teach Us About Democratic Resilience
excerpt: The period following the Civil War offers surprising lessons for how democracies recover — and how they backslide.
content:
On April 9, 1865, Robert E. Lee surrendered at Appomattox. The Civil War was over. But the harder question — what kind of nation America would become — was just beginning to be answered.

The Reconstruction era, roughly 1865 to 1877, is one of the most misunderstood periods in American history. For generations, it was taught as a cautionary tale of Northern overreach. More recent scholarship has revealed it as something far more complex: the first genuine attempt to build a multiracial democracy.

During Reconstruction, formerly enslaved people voted, held office, built schools, and established communities. The 13th, 14th, and 15th Amendments reshaped the Constitution. For a brief, remarkable moment, the promise of equality seemed within reach.

Then it collapsed. Through a combination of white supremacist violence, Northern fatigue, and political compromise, Reconstruction was abandoned. What followed — Jim Crow, disenfranchisement, decades of state-sanctioned racial terror — demonstrated how quickly democratic gains can be reversed when the political will to sustain them falters.

The lesson isn't that democracy is fragile — though it is. The lesson is that democracy requires active maintenance. Rights granted on paper must be defended in practice, generation after generation, or they will erode.

commentData.author: Dr. Caroline Hutchins
commentData.content: As a historian who teaches Reconstruction, thank you for this. I'd add that Eric Foner's work is essential reading here — his argument that Reconstruction was not a failure but an 'unfinished revolution' reframed the entire field. The parallels to voting rights challenges today are impossible to ignore.

commentData.author: James Okafor
commentData.content: The point about 'active maintenance' is the one that sticks with me. We talk about democracy like it's a state you achieve rather than a practice you sustain. That framing error has consequences.

title: The Two-Minute Reset
excerpt: When everything feels overwhelming, this simple practice can bring you back to the present moment.
content:
Here's a practice I've been doing for six years. It takes two minutes, and it works every time.

Stop what you're doing. Close your eyes. Take three slow breaths — in through the nose, out through the mouth. On each exhale, let your shoulders drop a little further.

Now ask yourself one question: "What is actually happening right now?" Not what happened this morning. Not what might happen tomorrow. What is happening right now, in this moment, in this room, in this body?

Usually, the answer is: nothing much. You're sitting in a chair. There's light coming through a window. You can hear the hum of a refrigerator or the distant sound of traffic. Your hands are warm. You're breathing.

That's it. Two minutes to remember that most of the chaos in your life exists in your thoughts about the past and future, not in the present moment. The present moment is almost always manageable.

title: The Case Against Dark Mode
excerpt: An unpopular opinion from a design studio: dark mode might be hurting readability more than it helps.
content:
We're going to lose some friends with this one.

Dark mode has become a default expectation in software design. Users demand it, designers implement it, and we've all collectively agreed that light text on dark backgrounds is easier on the eyes. But is it?

The research is surprisingly mixed. Studies on reading comprehension consistently show that dark text on light backgrounds produces faster reading speeds and better comprehension. This is called the "positive polarity advantage," and it's been replicated across dozens of studies since the 1980s.

The advantage comes from how our eyes process contrast. In light mode, the pupil constricts, increasing depth of field and sharpness. In dark mode, the pupil dilates, which can actually make text appear slightly blurred — an effect called "halation" where bright text bleeds into the dark background.

None of this means dark mode is bad. For ambient use — checking the time, scrolling a feed, watching video — dark mode is perfectly fine and easier on the eyes in low light. But for sustained reading? For actual comprehension of long-form text? Light mode may still be the better choice.

We're not saying remove dark mode. We're saying stop assuming it's universally superior. Design for both, and let users choose based on context, not ideology.

title: The Joy of Hyperlinks
excerpt: In an era of walled gardens and algorithm feeds, the humble hyperlink remains the web's most radical technology.
content:
I've been publishing links on this site for 26 years. That's not a typo. Twenty-six years of finding interesting things and pointing to them. And the core technology that makes it all work — the hyperlink — is as miraculous now as it was in 1998.

Think about what a link actually does. It says: "There is something over here that I think you should see." That's it. No algorithm decided you should see it. No engagement metric optimized its placement. A human being found something interesting and created a pathway to it. It's curation at its most fundamental.

The web was built on this idea — that anyone could point to anything, that knowledge could be connected in a vast, decentralized web of references. It was radical then and, in the age of platforms that want to keep you inside their walls, it's radical now.

Every time you publish a link to something you genuinely find interesting, you're participating in the web's original promise. You're saying: the internet is bigger than any one platform, and the best way to experience it is to follow the links.

title: What I Learned from Interviewing 200 Engineering Managers
excerpt: Patterns emerge when you talk to enough people. Here are the five things great engineering managers do differently.
content:
Over the past two years, I've interviewed more than 200 engineering managers for this newsletter. Directors, VPs, CTOs, first-time leads — across startups, scale-ups, and Big Tech. Patterns emerged.

First, the best managers protect their team's time ruthlessly. They attend the meetings so their engineers don't have to. They push back on scope creep before it reaches the team. They are a shield, not a funnel.

Second, they make decisions at the right altitude. They don't micromanage implementation details, but they also don't abdicate all technical decisions to the team. They've developed a sense for which decisions are reversible (let the team decide) and which are not (weigh in early).

Third, they give feedback in real time. Not in quarterly reviews. Not in annual performance cycles. In the moment, directly, with specificity.

Fourth, they invest in their own manager relationship. The best ICs-turned-managers I spoke with all said the same thing: managing up is not political — it's essential.

Fifth — and this surprised me — they read. Books on management, psychology, organizational design. Not blog posts and tweet threads. Books. The depth of understanding that comes from reading a full book on a topic simply can't be replicated by skimming summaries.

title: The AI Value Chain Is Inverting
excerpt: For the first time in tech history, the application layer may capture more value than the infrastructure layer.
content:
In every previous technology wave — PCs, the internet, mobile, cloud — the pattern was the same. Infrastructure captured the lion's share of value. Intel and Microsoft won PCs. AWS won cloud. The picks-and-shovels strategy was reliable.

AI is different, and I think most analysts are missing why.

The infrastructure layer in AI — GPUs, training compute, foundation models — is commoditizing faster than anyone expected. NVIDIA still has a commanding lead, but AMD, Google's TPUs, and custom silicon from Amazon and Microsoft are closing the gap.

Meanwhile, the application layer is showing unusual stickiness. Products like Cursor, Harvey, and Glean aren't just thin wrappers around GPT — they've built deep domain-specific workflows, proprietary data flywheels, and user habits that are genuinely hard to replicate.

This is the inversion: in AI, the scarce resource isn't compute or even model quality. It's understanding a specific workflow deeply enough to build an AI product that's 10x better than the general-purpose alternative.

title: The Fermi Paradox: A New Explanation
excerpt: After a decade of thinking about why we haven't found alien life, I have a new theory — and it's about timing, not distance.
content:
The Fermi Paradox — the contradiction between the high probability of extraterrestrial civilizations and the lack of evidence for them — has haunted me since I first encountered it in college. I've written about it several times on this blog. But I have a new thought.

Most explanations focus on space: the universe is too big, the distances too vast, the signals too faint. Or they focus on filters: civilizations destroy themselves, or they hide, or they transcend physical form.

But what if the answer is about time?

The universe is 13.8 billion years old. Earth-like planets have been forming for perhaps 10 billion years. But complex life on Earth took 4 billion years to develop, and technological civilization has existed for roughly 100 years. That's a ratio of 40 million to one.

Now consider: a technological civilization might last anywhere from a few hundred years to a few million years. Even generously, that's a window of a few million years out of a 10-billion-year timeline. The probability that two civilizations exist simultaneously, within communication range, during their overlapping technological windows, is vanishingly small.

We might not be alone in the universe. We might just be alone right now.

commentData.author: Raj Anand
commentData.content: This is essentially the 'temporal Fermi paradox' and I think it's the most underrated explanation. The math is devastating when you actually work it out. Even if a million civilizations have existed across the Milky Way's history, the odds of two overlapping within signal range are astronomically small.

commentData.author: Dr. Lena Sjöberg
commentData.content: Astrobiologist here. I'd push back slightly — this assumes civilizations don't leave persistent signatures (Dyson spheres, megastructures, atmospheric modification of planets). The 'temporal' argument works for radio signals but not necessarily for physical artifacts, which could persist for billions of years. Still, a thought-provoking framing.

commentData.author: Mike Okonkwo
commentData.content: The line 'We might not be alone in the universe — we might just be alone right now' is genuinely one of the most haunting sentences I've read. Somehow both comforting and devastating.

title: The Chocolate Chip Cookie I've Been Working On for a Year
excerpt: One cookie. Twelve months. Forty-seven batches. This is the recipe I'm finally ready to share.
content:
I know what you're thinking: it's a chocolate chip cookie. How much development could it possibly need? The answer is: a year, apparently, if you're me.

The problem is that everyone wants something different from a chocolate chip cookie. Thin and crispy? Thick and gooey? Cakey and soft? Chewy with crisp edges? Each of these is a fundamentally different recipe, not a minor tweak.

I wanted all of it: crisp edges that shatter, a chewy middle that pulls apart in strands, chunks of chocolate (not chips) that pool into molten pockets, a hint of toffee flavor from browned butter, and a whisper of salt on top.

The key breakthroughs: brown butter (cooled until solid, then creamed), a 36-hour cold rest, a mix of bread flour and all-purpose for chew, and a tablespoon of cornstarch, which keeps the center impossibly soft even as the edges crisp.

But the real secret? Slightly underbake them. Pull them at 11 minutes when they look not-quite-done. They'll set up on the pan and land in exactly the right place: crisp, chewy, molten, perfect.

title: Why Your Text Editor Is a Design Tool
excerpt: The best interface design starts not in Figma but in a plain text file. Here's our process.
content:
At iA, we start every design project the same way: by writing. Not wireframing. Not sketching. Writing.

This might seem backwards for a design studio, but there's a method to it. When you write out what a screen should communicate — in plain language, in a text editor — you're forced to think about information hierarchy before you think about visual hierarchy.

A text file has no fonts, no colors, no layout. It has only structure and content. And structure and content are the foundation of every good interface. If your screen doesn't work as a text outline, it won't work as a visual design.

Try it. Open a text file and write out your next screen as if you were explaining it to someone over the phone. What's the headline? What are the key pieces of information? What actions can the user take?

You'll find that many design problems are actually content problems. The screen is confusing not because the layout is wrong, but because the words are unclear, or the information is in the wrong order, or there's too much of it.

title: Things I've Bookmarked Recently
excerpt: A collection of interesting links: the world's oldest known melody, a database of every NYC streetlight, and more.
content:
Some things I've been reading, watching, and thinking about this week:

The world's oldest known melody is a Hurrian hymn from around 1400 BCE, found on a clay tablet in what is now Syria. Someone has reconstructed it, and you can listen to it. It's haunting — music from 3,400 years ago, reaching across time.

New York City has a publicly accessible database of every single streetlight in the five boroughs. There are 315,000 of them. Someone mapped them all.

A photographer has been documenting the last remaining phone booths in America. There's something poignant about these relics — each one a small monument to a time when communication required a specific place.

Finally: a biologist discovered that certain species of fungi can "learn" to navigate mazes. Not through intelligence — they have no nervous system — but through a kind of distributed memory encoded in their growth patterns. I can't stop thinking about this as a metaphor.

title: The Golden Hour Myth
excerpt: Everyone chases golden hour. Here's why the best landscape photographers are shooting two hours earlier.
content:
If you've taken any photography course or watched any YouTube tutorial, you've heard it: shoot during golden hour. That warm, low-angle light in the hour after sunrise and before sunset. It's Photography 101.

And it's not wrong — golden hour light is beautiful. But if you only shoot golden hour, you're missing the most dramatic light of the day: the blue hour that precedes it.

Blue hour — roughly 30 to 45 minutes before sunrise — is when the sky becomes a deep, luminous blue that no filter can replicate. The light is soft and even, shadows are minimal, and there's often a quality of stillness in the air that translates directly into the image.

More importantly, blue hour is when atmospheric phenomena are most visible. Fog settles into valleys. Frost crystals catch the first indirect light. The boundary between night and day creates color gradients across the sky.

The catch? You have to be in position, set up, and ready to shoot in complete darkness. That means arriving 90 minutes before sunrise. But the images are worth it.

title: On Letting Things Be Enough
excerpt: The practice of recognizing when what you have, what you've done, and who you are is already sufficient.
content:
There's a moment in every day when I catch myself reaching for more. More productivity. More progress. More optimization. More.

And I've been practicing, for years now, the art of catching that reaching and gently letting it go. Not because ambition is wrong, but because the constant sense of insufficiency is a kind of suffering.

Today was a good day. Not because anything remarkable happened, but because I noticed the ordinary things: the way morning light falls on the kitchen counter, the sound of my daughter laughing at something on the phone, the satisfaction of a simple lunch eaten slowly.

These are not consolation prizes. These are not what you settle for when you can't have more. These are the actual substance of a good life.

Enough is not a destination. It's a practice. And like all practices, it gets easier — and more rewarding — the more you do it.

title: Ursula K. Le Guin on the Inner Child and the Duty of the Artist
excerpt: A rare 1982 interview reveals Le Guin's thoughts on imagination, responsibility, and why adults need fairy tales.
content:
In a 1982 interview with The Oregonian — largely forgotten until a reader recently sent me a scan of the original clipping — Ursula K. Le Guin said something that stopped me mid-sentence:

"The creative adult is the child who survived. The child who was not killed by the demands of the adult world, not suppressed into silence, not shamed into conformity. To be an artist is not a talent. It is a form of survival."

Le Guin was responding to a question about why she wrote science fiction and fantasy — genres often dismissed as childish. Her answer was characteristically precise: she didn't write for children. She wrote from the part of herself that was still a child.

This is a radical claim about the nature of creativity. It suggests that artistic expression is not something we learn but something we manage not to lose.

But Le Guin went further. She argued that the artist has a duty — not to entertain, not even to instruct, but to keep alive the capacity for wonder. "A society that can't imagine the future," she said, "will be destroyed by it."

title: The iPad at 16
excerpt: Sixteen years in, the iPad is the most versatile computer Apple makes — and still the most frustrating.
content:
The iPad turned sixteen this year. In human terms, it's a teenager — full of potential, occasionally brilliant, frequently frustrating, and still figuring out what it wants to be when it grows up.

The hardware has long since surpassed what most people need from a computer. The M-series iPads have more processing power than most laptops. The displays are gorgeous. The Apple Pencil is the best stylus ever made.

And yet. And yet the software continues to hold it back in ways that feel increasingly inexplicable. File management remains awkward. Multitasking, despite years of refinement, still requires a PhD in gesture memorization.

The most telling critique of iPadOS isn't that it can't do things — it's that doing things takes more steps than it should. What takes one action on a Mac often takes three on an iPad.

I still believe the iPad represents something important: a more approachable, more portable, more versatile form of computing. But after sixteen years, "represents" shouldn't still be the operative word. It should just be.
